1:
"All the commandments which I command thee this day shall ye observe to do, that ye may live, and multiply, and go in and possess the land which the LORD sware unto your fathers." The witnesses note the charge, often repeated, to observe and do all the commandments, that they may live and possess the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes it is repeated over and over to impress it on their minds and show its importance and necessity. The witnesses note that Henry marks the charge the same as before — to keep and do all God's commandments. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the commandments — the whole charge; the observe to do — obedience pressed again; the that ye may live — the way of life and increase; the the heart slow to keep — the duty needing constant pressing.

The witnesses agree the repetition of the charge underlines its weight: again and again Israel is bidden to "observe to do" all the commandments, that they might live, multiply, and possess the land sworn to the fathers; the frequent pressing of the same duty shows how easily it is forgotten and how needful its constant inculcation, the way of life and inheritance lying in obedience — God reiterating his command because the heart of man is slow to keep it.

So the witnesses make the verse observe to do: The witnesses note the all the commandments: the whole charge. The witnesses note the observe to do: obedience pressed again. The witnesses note the that ye may live: the way of life and increase. The witnesses note the the heart slow to keep: the duty needing constant pressing. The expositors observe the all the commandments: the whole charge. The expositors observe the observe to do: obedience pressed again. The expositors observe the that ye may live: the way of life and increase. The expositors observe the the heart slow to keep: the duty needing constant pressing. The believer is taught to mark the all the commandments, the observe to do, the that ye may live, the the heart slow to keep, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was again charged to observe all the commandments, that they might live and possess the land — the repetition showing how needful its constant pressing, the way of life lying in obedience.
2:
"And thou shalt remember all the way which the LORD thy God led thee these forty years in the wilderness, to humble thee, and to prove thee, to know what was in thine heart..." The witnesses note the call to remember the forty years' leading in the wilderness, sent to humble and prove them. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the fortieth year, God going before them in the pillar of cloud and fire and directing their way. The witnesses note that the leading was to humble and prove them, to know what was in their heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remember all the way — the forty years' leading; the to humble thee — bringing down pride; the to prove thee — testing the heart; the the trial of faith — affliction searching the spirit (James 1:3).

The witnesses agree the wilderness was God's school for the heart: the long leading was meant "to humble" and "to prove" them, to bring out and make known what was within — their faith or unbelief, their obedience or murmuring; the trials of the way were not aimless hardship but a discipline by which God searched and disciplined his people, teaching that the afflictions of the godly are a proving of the heart, sent in love to humble pride and reveal what manner of spirit is in them (cf. James 1:3).

So the witnesses make the verse humbled and proved: The witnesses note the remember all the way: the forty years' leading. The witnesses note the to humble thee: bringing down pride. The witnesses note the to prove thee: testing the heart. The witnesses note the the trial of faith: affliction searching the spirit (James 1:3). The expositors observe the remember all the way: the forty years' leading. The expositors observe the to humble thee: bringing down pride. The expositors observe the to prove thee: testing the heart. The expositors observe the the trial of faith: affliction searching the spirit (James 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the remember all the way, the to humble thee, the to prove thee, the the trial of faith, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was to remember the wilderness leading, sent to humble and prove them — the trials a discipline searching the heart, the afflictions of the godly a proving of faith, sent in love (James 1:3).
3:
"And he humbled thee, and suffered thee to hunger, and fed thee with manna... that he might make thee know that man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD doth man live." The witnesses note the great lesson of the manna — that man lives not by bread only but by every word of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes he humbled and afflicted them with want of bread. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the lesson — fed with manna, that he might make them know man lives not by bread only but by every word from the LORD's mouth. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the suffered thee to hunger — then fed with manna; the not by bread only — life not in the food itself; the but by every word — the LORD's word the true sustenance; the Christ's answer to Satan — man shall not live by bread alone (Matt. 4:4).

The witnesses agree this verse teaches the deepest lesson of the wilderness: by hunger and then the manna, God showed that life depends not on bread but on his word and will — that he can sustain by means or without them, and that the creature lives by the Creator's appointment, not by the food itself; our Lord wielded this very word against the tempter — "man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word... of God" (Matt. 4:4) — making it the believer's charter of trust, that God's word is the true sustenance of the soul and the ground of all provision.

So the witnesses make the verse not by bread alone: The witnesses note the suffered thee to hunger: then fed with manna. The witnesses note the not by bread only: life not in the food itself. The witnesses note the but by every word: the LORD's word the true sustenance. The witnesses note the Christ's answer to Satan: man shall not live by bread alone (Matt. 4:4). The expositors observe the suffered thee to hunger: then fed with manna. The expositors observe the not by bread only: life not in the food itself. The expositors observe the but by every word: the LORD's word the true sustenance. The expositors observe the Christ's answer to Satan: man shall not live by bread alone (Matt. 4:4). The believer is taught to mark the suffered thee to hunger, the not by bread only, the but by every word, the Christ's answer to Satan, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the manna taught man lives not by bread only but by every word of God — the deepest lesson of the wilderness, Christ's answer to the tempter, God's word the true sustenance of life (Matt. 4:4).
4:
"Thy raiment waxed not old upon thee, neither did thy foot swell, these forty years." The witnesses note God's preserving care — their clothes not wearing out, their feet not swelling, in forty years. The witnesses note that Gill notes they wanted not clothes all the forty years; some account for it by the rising generation supplied with the clothes of those who died and the spoils of Amalek, others by the much clothing brought out of Egypt. The witnesses note that their feet did not swell with the long journeying. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the raiment waxed not old — their clothes kept whole; the foot did not swell — the body preserved in travel; the forty years — a sustained, constant care; the every need watched — the smallest comfort not beneath God.

The witnesses agree the verse marks the tender, constant providence of God over the least things: through forty years their garments did not wear out nor their feet swell with travel, a sustained care that reached to clothing and body alike; whether by natural means or miracle, the point is God's unfailing provision for his people in the wilderness, that he who fed them with manna also kept their raiment whole — a witness that the Lord watches over every need of his own, the smallest comfort not beneath his care.

So the witnesses make the verse preserving care: The witnesses note the raiment waxed not old: their clothes kept whole. The witnesses note the foot did not swell: the body preserved in travel. The witnesses note the forty years: a sustained, constant care. The witnesses note the every need watched: the smallest comfort not beneath God. The expositors observe the raiment waxed not old: their clothes kept whole. The expositors observe the foot did not swell: the body preserved in travel. The expositors observe the forty years: a sustained, constant care. The expositors observe the every need watched: the smallest comfort not beneath God. The believer is taught to mark the raiment waxed not old, the foot did not swell, the forty years, the every need watched, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that their raiment did not wear out nor their feet swell in forty years — the tender, constant providence of God, who kept every need of his people in the wilderness, the smallest comfort not beneath his care.
5:
"Thou shalt also consider in thine heart, that, as a man chasteneth his son, so the LORD thy God chasteneth thee." The witnesses note the call to consider that God's dealings were a fatherly chastening, as a man chastens his son. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were frequently to think of and weigh in their hearts this matter of great moment, for their peace, comfort, and the glory of God. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the comparison — as a man chastens his son, so the LORD chastens. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the consider in thine heart — weigh this great matter; the as a man chasteneth his son — a father's discipline; the so the LORD chasteneth — his dealings in love; the the proof of sonship — whom he loves he chastens (Heb. 12:6).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the gracious meaning of the wilderness trials: they were a father's chastening, not a judge's wrath — God dealing with Israel "as a man chasteneth his son," for their good and not their ruin; the truth is the comfort of every afflicted believer, that the discipline of God is the proof of his fatherhood, "for whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth" (Heb. 12:6), the rod in love and the trials a training, that his children be humbled, taught, and made partakers of his holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse a father's chastening: The witnesses note the consider in thine heart: weigh this great matter. The witnesses note the as a man chasteneth his son: a father's discipline. The witnesses note the so the LORD chasteneth: his dealings in love. The witnesses note the the proof of sonship: whom he loves he chastens (Heb. 12:6). The expositors observe the consider in thine heart: weigh this great matter. The expositors observe the as a man chasteneth his son: a father's discipline. The expositors observe the so the LORD chasteneth: his dealings in love. The expositors observe the the proof of sonship: whom he loves he chastens (Heb. 12:6). The believer is taught to mark the consider in thine heart, the as a man chasteneth his son, the so the LORD chasteneth, the the proof of sonship, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God chastened Israel as a man his son — the wilderness trials a father's discipline, the proof of his love, the rod in kindness training his children (Heb. 12:6).
6:
"Therefore thou shalt keep the commandments of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, and to fear him." The witnesses note the conclusion — therefore keep his commandments, walk in his ways, and fear him. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to keep his commands not only because they are his, a covenant God and Father's, but because he had dealt so bountifully with them, providing food and raiment and afflicting them in love. The witnesses note that they were to walk in his ways and fear him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the commandments — the practical conclusion; the walk in his ways — the path of obedience; the and fear him — reverent service; the gratitude the spring — obedience drawn from his bounty.

The witnesses agree the "therefore" draws obedience from all God's gracious dealings: because he had fed, clothed, and lovingly chastened them, Israel was bound to keep his commandments, walk in his ways, and fear him; the argument shows that gratitude is the spring of obedience, the remembrance of God's goodness and fatherly care moving the heart to serve him — duty flowing not from constraint but from the sense of mercies received, the bountiful God claiming the love and obedience of those he has so kept.

So the witnesses make the verse therefore keep them: The witnesses note the keep the commandments: the practical conclusion. The witnesses note the walk in his ways: the path of obedience. The witnesses note the and fear him: reverent service. The witnesses note the gratitude the spring: obedience drawn from his bounty. The expositors observe the keep the commandments: the practical conclusion. The expositors observe the walk in his ways: the path of obedience. The expositors observe the and fear him: reverent service. The expositors observe the gratitude the spring: obedience drawn from his bounty. The believer is taught to mark the keep the commandments, the walk in his ways, the and fear him, the gratitude the spring, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was therefore to keep his commandments, walk in his ways, and fear him — gratitude the spring of obedience, the remembrance of God's bounty and fatherly care moving the heart to serve him.
7:
"For the LORD thy God bringeth thee into a good land, a land of brooks of water, of fountains and depths that spring out of valleys and hills;" The witnesses note the goodness of the land God was bringing them into — well-watered with brooks, fountains, and springs. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Canaan abounded with good things, a land flowing with milk and honey, with plenty for convenience and delight — another reason to serve the Lord. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the good land of brooks and fountains. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a good land — rich in every good thing; the brooks of water — well-watered and fruitful; the fountains and depths — springing from valleys and hills; the the gift a motive — its richness binding them to the Giver.

The witnesses agree the description of the good land is set as a fresh motive to obedience: God was bringing them into a country rich in water — brooks, fountains, and springs — a land of plenty and delight, far better than the dry wilderness; the goodness of the gift is made a ground of duty, that a people brought into such abundance owe their service to the Giver, the very richness of the inheritance binding them the more to love and obey the God who bestowed it.

So the witnesses make the verse a good land: The witnesses note the a good land: rich in every good thing. The witnesses note the brooks of water: well-watered and fruitful. The witnesses note the fountains and depths: springing from valleys and hills. The witnesses note the the gift a motive: its richness binding them to the Giver. The expositors observe the a good land: rich in every good thing. The expositors observe the brooks of water: well-watered and fruitful. The expositors observe the fountains and depths: springing from valleys and hills. The expositors observe the the gift a motive: its richness binding them to the Giver. The believer is taught to mark the a good land, the brooks of water, the fountains and depths, the the gift a motive, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God was bringing them into a good, well-watered land — its richness a fresh motive to obedience, a people brought into such abundance owing their service to the Giver.
8:
"A land of wheat, and barley, and vines, and fig trees, and pomegranates; a land of oil olive, and honey;" The witnesses note the fruitfulness of the land — its grains, vines, and fruits in abundance. The witnesses note that Gill notes there were two harvests, barley beginning at the passover and wheat at Pentecost, with great plenty, instanced in Solomon's household and his gifts to Hiram. The witnesses note that the land yielded vines, figs, pomegranates, oil, and honey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wheat and barley — two harvests of grain; the vines, figs, pomegranates — rich fruit in plenty; the oil and honey — all for food and delight; the God's lavish provision — the land a witness to his bounty.

The witnesses agree the listing of the land's seven kinds of produce magnifies its rich fruitfulness: wheat and barley, vines and figs and pomegranates, oil and honey — a land yielding all that heart could wish for food and delight; the abundance, far surpassing the manna of the desert, was God's lavish provision for his people in their rest, the goodness of the land a continual witness to the bounty of the God who gave it, and a call to thankful enjoyment of his gifts.

So the witnesses make the verse the fruitful land: The witnesses note the wheat and barley: two harvests of grain. The witnesses note the vines, figs, pomegranates: rich fruit in plenty. The witnesses note the oil and honey: all for food and delight. The witnesses note the God's lavish provision: the land a witness to his bounty. The expositors observe the wheat and barley: two harvests of grain. The expositors observe the vines, figs, pomegranates: rich fruit in plenty. The expositors observe the oil and honey: all for food and delight. The expositors observe the God's lavish provision: the land a witness to his bounty. The believer is taught to mark the wheat and barley, the vines, figs, pomegranates, the oil and honey, the God's lavish provision, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the land yielded wheat, vines, figs, oil, and honey — rich fruitfulness far surpassing the manna, God's lavish provision for his people, the goodness of the land a witness to his bounty.
9:
"A land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness, thou shalt not lack any thing in it; a land whose stones are iron, and out of whose hills thou mayest dig brass." The witnesses note the land's fulness — bread without scarceness and metals in its very stones. The witnesses note that Gill notes they should have plenty of all sorts of provisions, bread often put for all. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the stones impregnated with iron, and brass dug from the hills — the most useful of metals. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bread without scarceness — plenty of provision; the not lack any thing — every want supplied; the stones are iron — metal in the very ground; the the complete gift — food above and metal below, lacking nothing.

The witnesses agree the land's wealth reached from the surface to the depths: it gave bread without scarceness above and iron and brass below, lacking nothing for the life and labour of its people; the completeness of the provision — food for the body and metal for the tools of work and war — shows the thoroughness of God's gift, a land furnished with all that a settled people could need, that they should "not lack any thing in it," the fulness of the inheritance answering every want.

So the witnesses make the verse lacking nothing: The witnesses note the bread without scarceness: plenty of provision. The witnesses note the not lack any thing: every want supplied. The witnesses note the stones are iron: metal in the very ground. The witnesses note the the complete gift: food above and metal below, lacking nothing. The expositors observe the bread without scarceness: plenty of provision. The expositors observe the not lack any thing: every want supplied. The expositors observe the stones are iron: metal in the very ground. The expositors observe the the complete gift: food above and metal below, lacking nothing. The believer is taught to mark the bread without scarceness, the not lack any thing, the stones are iron, the the complete gift, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the land gave bread without scarceness and iron and brass from its hills — the completeness of God's gift, a land furnished with all a people could need, lacking nothing.
10:
"When thou hast eaten and art full, then thou shalt bless the LORD thy God for the good land which he hath given thee." The witnesses note the duty of thanksgiving — when filled, to bless the LORD for the good land. The witnesses note that Gill notes that as God would furnish them with plenty, they might eat liberally, provided not to intemperance; this shows we are to return thanks for a plentiful meal as well as ask a blessing on it. The witnesses note that the thanks were for the good land God had given. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the when thou art full — being filled with plenty; the bless the LORD — the duty of thanksgiving; the for the good land — owning him the Giver; the plenty sanctified — prosperity an occasion of praise (1 Tim. 4:4).

The witnesses agree the verse enjoins the grace of thankfulness amid plenty: being filled, they were to "bless the LORD" for the good land, owning him the Giver of all they enjoyed; the duty of returning thanks for a plentiful meal, as Gill marks, is the appointed answer to God's bounty, the heart that eats and is full bound to lift up praise — a holy use of plenty, that prosperity become an occasion of thanksgiving rather than forgetfulness, every good gift acknowledged to the Father of lights (cf. 1 Tim. 4:4).

So the witnesses make the verse bless the LORD: The witnesses note the when thou art full: being filled with plenty. The witnesses note the bless the LORD: the duty of thanksgiving. The witnesses note the for the good land: owning him the Giver. The witnesses note the plenty sanctified: prosperity an occasion of praise (1 Tim. 4:4). The expositors observe the when thou art full: being filled with plenty. The expositors observe the bless the LORD: the duty of thanksgiving. The expositors observe the for the good land: owning him the Giver. The expositors observe the plenty sanctified: prosperity an occasion of praise (1 Tim. 4:4). The believer is taught to mark the when thou art full, the bless the LORD, the for the good land, the plenty sanctified, and to remember the LORD his God. The verse thus records that being full, they were to bless the LORD for the good land — the grace of thankfulness amid plenty, prosperity made an occasion of praise, every good gift acknowledged to the Giver (1 Tim. 4:4).
11:
"Beware that thou forget not the LORD thy God, in not keeping his commandments, and his judgments, and his statutes, which I command thee this day:" The witnesses note the central warning of the chapter — beware of forgetting the LORD by ceasing to keep his commands. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD is the Father of mercies and fountain of goodness, the author of every good gift; plenty is apt to induce forgetfulness of God, when it should rather keep him in remembrance. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the warning to beware lest they forget. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the beware — the central warning; the forget not the LORD — the Father of mercies; the in not keeping his commandments — forgetting shown in disobedience; the the peril of plenty — abundance making men forget the Giver.

The witnesses agree this is the great peril against which the chapter warns, and the strange perversity of the heart: plenty, which ought to keep God in continual remembrance, is the very thing that makes men forget him; the forgetting is shown to be no mere lapse of memory but the ceasing to keep his commandments — a practical apostasy of the life; the warning teaches that the danger of prosperity is real and subtle, and that the only safeguard is a heart that remembers the Giver in the gifts and proves its memory by obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse forget not the LORD: The witnesses note the beware: the central warning. The witnesses note the forget not the LORD: the Father of mercies. The witnesses note the in not keeping his commandments: forgetting shown in disobedience. The witnesses note the the peril of plenty: abundance making men forget the Giver. The expositors observe the beware: the central warning. The expositors observe the forget not the LORD: the Father of mercies. The expositors observe the in not keeping his commandments: forgetting shown in disobedience. The expositors observe the the peril of plenty: abundance making men forget the Giver. The believer is taught to mark the beware, the forget not the LORD, the in not keeping his commandments, the the peril of plenty, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that Israel was to beware of forgetting the LORD by ceasing to keep his commandments — the subtle peril of plenty, the only safeguard a heart that remembers the Giver and proves it by obedience.
12:
"Lest when thou hast eaten and art full, and hast built goodly houses, and dwelt therein;" The witnesses note the circumstances of the danger — being full, with goodly houses built and settled in. The witnesses note that Gill notes the danger of being continually full, day after day, indulged with great plenty. The witnesses note that Ellicott recalls Jonadab's charge to the Rechabites to dwell in tents, that they might live long as strangers in the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eaten and art full — a full table; the goodly houses — fine dwellings built; the and dwelt therein — settled in ease; the peril in security — the soul watchful when most at ease.

The witnesses agree the verse names the very comforts that breed forgetfulness: a full table, fine houses, and settled ease are the occasions in which the heart most readily lifts itself and forgets God; Ellicott's recollection of the Rechabites, bidden to dwell in tents lest settled abundance corrupt them, points the same lesson — that prosperity and security are spiritually perilous, and the soul must be most watchful when most at ease, lest the gifts of God become a snare and the comforts of the world crowd out the remembrance of him.

So the witnesses make the verse the comforts of ease: The witnesses note the eaten and art full: a full table. The witnesses note the goodly houses: fine dwellings built. The witnesses note the and dwelt therein: settled in ease. The witnesses note the peril in security: the soul watchful when most at ease. The expositors observe the eaten and art full: a full table. The expositors observe the goodly houses: fine dwellings built. The expositors observe the and dwelt therein: settled in ease. The expositors observe the peril in security: the soul watchful when most at ease. The believer is taught to mark the eaten and art full, the goodly houses, the and dwelt therein, the peril in security, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that the danger lay in being full, with goodly houses and settled ease — the very comforts that breed forgetfulness, the soul to be most watchful when most at ease, lest prosperity become a snare.
13:
"And when thy herds and thy flocks multiply, and thy silver and thy gold is multiplied, and all that thou hast is multiplied;" The witnesses note the multiplying of herds, silver, and gold — the increase of wealth that tries the heart. The witnesses note that Gill notes the herds and flocks multiplying, having good pasture in so fruitful a land. The witnesses note that all they had would be multiplied. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the herds and flocks multiply — increase of cattle; the silver and gold — riches heaped up; the all... multiplied — abundance growing; the temptation growing — the heart to hold faster as hands fill (Ps. 62:10).

The witnesses agree the heaping-up of increase — flocks, silver, gold, "all that thou hast" multiplied — is named as the rising tide that may sweep the heart from God: the more the wealth, the greater the danger of pride and forgetfulness; the verse traces the growth of abundance step by step, that Israel might watch the peril growing with the riches, knowing that the multiplying of goods is a multiplying of temptation, and that the heart must hold faster to God the fuller its hands become (cf. Ps. 62:10, if riches increase, set not your heart upon them).

So the witnesses make the verse wealth multiplied: The witnesses note the herds and flocks multiply: increase of cattle. The witnesses note the silver and gold: riches heaped up. The witnesses note the all... multiplied: abundance growing. The witnesses note the temptation growing: the heart to hold faster as hands fill (Ps. 62:10). The expositors observe the herds and flocks multiply: increase of cattle. The expositors observe the silver and gold: riches heaped up. The expositors observe the all... multiplied: abundance growing. The expositors observe the temptation growing: the heart to hold faster as hands fill (Ps. 62:10). The believer is taught to mark the herds and flocks multiply, the silver and gold, the all... multiplied, the temptation growing, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that herds, silver, and gold would multiply — the rising tide of wealth that tries the heart, the multiplying of goods a multiplying of temptation, the heart to hold faster to God as the hands fill (Ps. 62:10).
14:
"Then thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD thy God, which brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage;" The witnesses note the root of the danger — the heart lifted up in pride, forgetting the LORD who redeemed them. The witnesses note that Gill notes the heart is apt to be lifted up when riches increase. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the pride of receiving these things as if by one's own wisdom, valour, and industry, or for one's own merit. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thine heart lifted up — the pride of riches; the forget the LORD — ascribing wealth to self; the who brought thee from Egypt — the Redeemer forgotten; the pride the root — filled and the heart exalted (Hos. 13:6).

The witnesses agree the heart of the peril is pride: when riches increase, the heart is "lifted up," and forgets the LORD, ascribing its wealth to its own wisdom and worth; Wesley names the very lie of the proud heart — that it received and earned all by itself — over against the truth that all came from God who redeemed them from bondage; the warning lays bare that self-exaltation is the root of forgetting God, and that humility, owning all as his gift, is the only guard of the heart in prosperity (cf. Hos. 13:6, they were filled, and their heart was exalted; therefore have they forgotten me).

So the witnesses make the verse the lifted-up heart: The witnesses note the thine heart lifted up: the pride of riches. The witnesses note the forget the LORD: ascribing wealth to self. The witnesses note the who brought thee from Egypt: the Redeemer forgotten. The witnesses note the pride the root: filled and the heart exalted (Hos. 13:6). The expositors observe the thine heart lifted up: the pride of riches. The expositors observe the forget the LORD: ascribing wealth to self. The expositors observe the who brought thee from Egypt: the Redeemer forgotten. The expositors observe the pride the root: filled and the heart exalted (Hos. 13:6). The believer is taught to mark the thine heart lifted up, the forget the LORD, the who brought thee from Egypt, the pride the root, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that the lifted-up heart would forget the LORD who redeemed them — pride the root of forgetting God, ascribing all to self, humility the only guard, lest being filled the heart be exalted and forget him (Hos. 13:6).
15:
"Who led thee through that great and terrible wilderness, wherein were fiery serpents, and scorpions, and drought, where there was no water; who brought thee forth water out of the rock of flint;" The witnesses note the recalling of God's care through the terrible wilderness — its serpents, drought, and the water from the rock. The witnesses note that Gill notes the wilderness of Paran, great and terrible, with fiery serpents and scorpions, and the water brought from the rock. The witnesses note that Calvin recalls the water brought from the flinty rock when the people suffered thirst. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the terrible wilderness — serpents, scorpions, drought; the led thee through — god's preserving care; the water from the rock — life given in the barren place; the that Rock was Christ — grace sustaining through the wilderness (1 Cor. 10:4).

The witnesses agree the verse heaps up the perils God brought them safely through, that they might not forget him: the great and terrible wilderness, its fiery serpents and scorpions, its drought — and through it all God led them and even brought water from the flinty rock; the recital magnifies the care that preserved them where nature offered only death, and the rock that gave water in the desert points beyond itself, for "that Rock was Christ" (1 Cor. 10:4), the supply of life in the barren place a figure of the grace that sustains his people through every wilderness.

So the witnesses make the verse through the wilderness: The witnesses note the the terrible wilderness: serpents, scorpions, drought. The witnesses note the led thee through: God's preserving care. The witnesses note the water from the rock: life given in the barren place. The witnesses note the that Rock was Christ: grace sustaining through the wilderness (1 Cor. 10:4). The expositors observe the the terrible wilderness: serpents, scorpions, drought. The expositors observe the led thee through: God's preserving care. The expositors observe the water from the rock: life given in the barren place. The expositors observe the that Rock was Christ: grace sustaining through the wilderness (1 Cor. 10:4). The believer is taught to mark the the terrible wilderness, the led thee through, the water from the rock, the that Rock was Christ, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that God led them through the terrible wilderness and brought water from the rock — his care preserving where nature offered death, the rock a figure of Christ, the supply of life in the barren place (1 Cor. 10:4).
16:
"Who fed thee in the wilderness with manna, which thy fathers knew not, that he might humble thee, and that he might prove thee, to do thee good at thy latter end;" The witnesses note the purpose of the wilderness discipline — to humble and prove them, that he might do them good at the last. The witnesses note that Gill notes he fed them with manna all the forty years, which their fathers knew not. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the miracle of the manna (Exod. 16:13). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fed thee with manna — the wilderness provision; the humble and prove — the discipline of the way; the to do thee good — at thy latter end; the affliction's end — the peaceable fruit of righteousness (Heb. 12:11).

The witnesses agree the verse unveils the gracious end of all the hard dealings: the humbling and proving of the wilderness were "to do thee good at thy latter end," the discipline aimed at a blessed issue; God afflicted to bless, humbled to exalt, that the trials of the way might ripen into good at the last — a truth that lights up every dark providence, that the chastening of God works "the peaceable fruit of righteousness" (Heb. 12:11), and that he leads his people through hardship to a good and blessed end.

So the witnesses make the verse to do thee good: The witnesses note the fed thee with manna: the wilderness provision. The witnesses note the humble and prove: the discipline of the way. The witnesses note the to do thee good: at thy latter end. The witnesses note the affliction's end: the peaceable fruit of righteousness (Heb. 12:11). The expositors observe the fed thee with manna: the wilderness provision. The expositors observe the humble and prove: the discipline of the way. The expositors observe the to do thee good: at thy latter end. The expositors observe the affliction's end: the peaceable fruit of righteousness (Heb. 12:11). The believer is taught to mark the fed thee with manna, the humble and prove, the to do thee good, the affliction's end, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that God humbled and proved them to do them good at the last — the discipline aimed at a blessed issue, affliction working the peaceable fruit of righteousness, God leading his people through hardship to good (Heb. 12:11).
17:
"And thou say in thine heart, My power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth." The witnesses note the proud thought against which the chapter warns — that one's own power had gained the wealth. The witnesses note that Gill notes these words connect with the warning, the secret boast of the heart. The witnesses note that the danger was ascribing their prosperity to their own strength and labour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the say in thine heart — the secret boast; the my power and might — self-crediting pride; the hath gotten me this wealth — forgetting the Giver; the the lie exposed — what hast thou that thou didst not receive (1 Cor. 4:7).

The witnesses agree the verse names the very lie the heart whispers in prosperity: "my power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth" — the proud self-crediting that forgets God and worships the creature's own strength; the warning exposes the root of all forgetting, that man takes to himself the glory of what God gave, the secret boast of self-sufficiency; against it stands the apostle's question, "what hast thou that thou didst not receive?" (1 Cor. 4:7), the rebuke of all pride that claims its blessings as its own achievement.

So the witnesses make the verse my power got this: The witnesses note the say in thine heart: the secret boast. The witnesses note the my power and might: self-crediting pride. The witnesses note the hath gotten me this wealth: forgetting the Giver. The witnesses note the the lie exposed: what hast thou that thou didst not receive (1 Cor. 4:7). The expositors observe the say in thine heart: the secret boast. The expositors observe the my power and might: self-crediting pride. The expositors observe the hath gotten me this wealth: forgetting the Giver. The expositors observe the the lie exposed: what hast thou that thou didst not receive (1 Cor. 4:7). The believer is taught to mark the say in thine heart, the my power and might, the hath gotten me this wealth, the the lie exposed, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that the proud heart says "my power... hath gotten me this wealth" — the lie of self-sufficiency that forgets God, rebuked by the truth, what hast thou that thou didst not receive (1 Cor. 4:7).
18:
"But thou shalt remember the LORD thy God: for it is he that giveth thee power to get wealth, that he may establish his covenant which he sware unto thy fathers..." The witnesses note the cure for the proud thought — to remember that God gives the very power to get wealth. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to remember the LORD, the author of their beings and the God of their mercies, who had done such great things for them and given them the fruitful land. The witnesses note that Clarke notes God gives the power to get wealth. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remember the LORD — the cure for pride; the he giveth thee power — even the ability to gain; the to get wealth — the strength itself his gift; the to establish his covenant — the wealth for his gracious end (James 1:17).

The witnesses agree the answer to pride is remembrance of God as the source even of our ability: not only the wealth but "the power to get wealth" is his gift, so that the strength and skill men boast of are themselves from God; the truth strikes at the root of self-glory, for if even our powers are given, no boast remains — and the gift is for a gracious end, to establish his covenant, the wealth granted that his promise to the fathers be fulfilled; all is of grace, the means and the end alike his (cf. James 1:17).

So the witnesses make the verse God gives the power: The witnesses note the remember the LORD: the cure for pride. The witnesses note the he giveth thee power: even the ability to gain. The witnesses note the to get wealth: the strength itself his gift. The witnesses note the to establish his covenant: the wealth for his gracious end (James 1:17). The expositors observe the remember the LORD: the cure for pride. The expositors observe the he giveth thee power: even the ability to gain. The expositors observe the to get wealth: the strength itself his gift. The expositors observe the to establish his covenant: the wealth for his gracious end (James 1:17). The believer is taught to mark the remember the LORD, the he giveth thee power, the to get wealth, the to establish his covenant, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that they were to remember God gives the very power to get wealth — the cure for pride, even our abilities his gift, the wealth granted to establish his covenant, all of grace (James 1:17).
19:
"And it shall be, if thou do at all forget the LORD thy God, and walk after other gods, and serve them, and worship them, I testify against you this day that ye shall surely perish." The witnesses note the solemn warning — that forgetting God for idols would bring sure destruction. The witnesses note that Gill notes forgetting God is either not acknowledging his mercies but ascribing them to oneself, or forsaking his worship and serving other gods. The witnesses note that Moses testifies they shall surely perish. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if thou forget the LORD — turning from him; the walk after other gods — to idolatry; the I testify against you — the solemn witness; the ye shall surely perish — apostasy ending in ruin.

The witnesses agree the warning rises to its height in a solemn testimony of doom: to forget the LORD and turn to other gods would surely bring destruction, Moses calling heaven to witness against them; the forgetting feared is no small thing but a turning of the whole life to idols, and its end is ruin — the strongest motive to faithfulness, that apostasy is not merely loss but death, the same judgment that fell on the nations awaiting Israel if they walked in their idolatry, God no respecter of persons in the punishment of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse surely perish: The witnesses note the if thou forget the LORD: turning from him. The witnesses note the walk after other gods: to idolatry. The witnesses note the I testify against you: the solemn witness. The witnesses note the ye shall surely perish: apostasy ending in ruin. The expositors observe the if thou forget the LORD: turning from him. The expositors observe the walk after other gods: to idolatry. The expositors observe the I testify against you: the solemn witness. The expositors observe the ye shall surely perish: apostasy ending in ruin. The believer is taught to mark the if thou forget the LORD, the walk after other gods, the I testify against you, the ye shall surely perish, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that forgetting God for idols would bring sure destruction — the solemn testimony of doom, apostasy ending not in loss only but death, the strongest motive to faithfulness.
20:
"As the nations which the LORD destroyeth before your face, so shall ye perish; because ye would not be obedient unto the voice of the LORD your God." The witnesses note that disobedient Israel would perish like the very nations God was destroying before them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they would be cut off or cast out as the nations were, the same sins, particularly idolatry, being committed — understood of the seven nations God was gradually destroying. The witnesses note that this would be because they would not obey the voice of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as the nations — destroyed for idolatry; the so shall ye perish — israel under the same rule; the because not obedient — their disobedience the cause; the no respecter of persons — privilege no shield from judgment (Rom. 2:11).

The witnesses agree the closing warning levels Israel with the heathen if they sin like them: the nations were being destroyed for their idolatry, and Israel, committing the same sin, would "so perish" — their election no shield if they forsook their God; the truth is searching, that privilege does not exempt from judgment but rather heightens responsibility, and that God will deal with his own people by the same rule of righteousness, cutting off the disobedient as he cuts off the heathen, for he is no respecter of persons (cf. Rom. 2:11).

So the witnesses make the verse perish like the nations: The witnesses note the as the nations: destroyed for idolatry. The witnesses note the so shall ye perish: Israel under the same rule. The witnesses note the because not obedient: their disobedience the cause. The witnesses note the no respecter of persons: privilege no shield from judgment (Rom. 2:11). The expositors observe the as the nations: destroyed for idolatry. The expositors observe the so shall ye perish: Israel under the same rule. The expositors observe the because not obedient: their disobedience the cause. The expositors observe the no respecter of persons: privilege no shield from judgment (Rom. 2:11). The believer is taught to mark the as the nations, the so shall ye perish, the because not obedient, the no respecter of persons, and to remember the LORD who gives all. The verse thus records that disobedient Israel would perish like the nations God destroyed — their election no shield, God dealing with his own by the same righteousness, no respecter of persons (Rom. 2:11).
